# The Stationary Gravitational Field

Once the source has been packaged, the next question is how that source deforms the stage. This chapter presents the main achieved field package of Part III.

The emphasis is deliberately narrow. The goal is not to write down every possible gravitational equation the framework might later contain. The goal is to state the smallest stationary field system that is already stable enough to carry the current overlap with ordinary weak and moderate gravitational physics, to introduce the field variables the reader actually needs, and to show why the scalar-well and shift split is the right organizing grammar.

## The stationary field variables

In the present stationary regime, the field layer is organized around three objects.

The first is the scalar field $\theta_0(x)$, which packages the depth or well structure of the gravitational background. The second is the shift potential $A_i(x)$, which packages the directional and rotational part of the background. The third is the spatial metric sector $\gamma_{ij}$, which is real and important but, in the present mainline package, is not the lead actor.

The scalar packaging most directly used by the reader is

$$
G(x) = \sqrt{1 - 2\theta_0(x)}.
$$

This quantity is the local gravitational clock factor. It tells us how the stationary background dresses the local fermic scale and therefore how the gravitational context enters later observer bookkeeping.

The shift structure is carried by $A_i$. In earlier correspondence notes it is sometimes convenient to write $A_i = -\theta_i$, but for the present chapter it is clearer to treat $A_i$ directly as the operational shift field. Its curl-like content may be written as

$$
B_{ij} = \partial_i A_j - \partial_j A_i,
$$

which measures the rotational or flux-like part of the stationary background.

The conceptual division is the important one. The scalar field controls the well or lapse-like side of the stationary geometry. The shift field controls the directional and frame-drag side. Keeping them distinct is what allows the later observer story to remain intelligible.

## The minimal stationary equation block

The current stationary correspondence scaffold can be stated in one compact equation block. In the operational overlap regime,

$$
\Delta \theta_0 = -\frac{4\pi G_N}{c^2}\rho,
$$

$$
\Delta A_i = \frac{16\pi G_N}{c^3}j_i^\perp,
\qquad
\partial_i A_i = 0.
$$

Here $\rho$ is the effective scalar source density, $j_i^\perp$ is the transverse part of the stationary current, and the gauge condition keeps the shift sector in its simplest stationary form.

This block is not being presented as the final native gravity theory. It is being presented as the minimal stationary field scaffold that is already stable enough to reuse. It captures the even-to-scalar and odd-to-shift logic in the regime where the current correspondence claims are strongest.

The chapter is intentionally conservative about what it asks this block to do. It is enough that it reproduces the Newtonian limit cleanly, gives a controlled weak and moderate stationary dictionary, and keeps the shift sector visible without burying the narrative in gauge and coefficient audits.

## Scalar well, potential, and the Newtonian limit

To read the scalar channel in ordinary gravitational language, define the Newtonian potential

$$
U := -c^2\theta_0.
$$

Then the scalar field equation becomes

$$
\Delta U = 4\pi G_N\rho.
$$

This is the standard Poisson form, and it fixes the sign convention the reader should carry through the rest of the part. In particular,

$$
a = -\nabla U,
$$

so positive source density produces the expected inward acceleration.

Outside a static spherical source with total mass $M$, the solution reduces to

$$
U(r) = -\frac{G_N M}{r}.
$$

That is the familiar weak static result, but the interpretation here matters. The framework is not saying merely that one can imitate Newtonian gravity. It is saying that the scalar channel of the parity-split source grammar naturally produces the stationary well structure that the Newtonian limit requires.

The packaged clock factor then becomes

$$
G = \sqrt{1 - 2\theta_0} = \sqrt{1 + 2U/c^2},
$$

which is the object that later feeds local clock dressing and observer bookkeeping.

## The shift sector and stationary rotation

A purely static source is not the whole story. Once the source carries directional imbalance, the field acquires a genuine shift structure. The operational equation

$$
\Delta A_i = \frac{16\pi G_N}{c^3}j_i^\perp
$$

is the first stationary expression of that fact.

This is the channel that later produces frame-drag-like behavior. It is sourced not by the even scalar content, but by the odd or current-bearing content of the source. That separation is one of the cleanest structural features of the present M1 gravity package. The scalar well is not asked to carry the whole burden of gravity. The directional background structure has its own channel.

In a weak far zone around a bounded rotating source, the shift field scales in the way one expects from a frame-drag sector. That does not yet amount to a finished native rotating-source derivation, but it is enough to justify the reader-facing claim that stationary rotation belongs in the odd channel rather than being a small decorative correction on top of the scalar one.

## Weak and moderate stationary dictionary

The stationary field package becomes GR-readable through a deliberately narrow dictionary. In the present overlap regime,

$$
g_{00} = -(1 + 2U/c^2),
\qquad
g_{0i} = A_i/c.
$$

These relations are the ones the reader needs most. The first identifies the scalar well with the usual weak-field lapse sector. The second identifies the shift potential with the metric's frame-drag or mixed time-space sector.

The spatial metric sector $g_{ij}$, or equivalently the fully developed $\gamma_{ij}$ story, is not denied here. It is deferred. That deferral is intentional. The current mainline achievement is strongest when read through the scalar and shift packaging first. Once that package is stable, the spatial sector can be added with better discipline.

This is therefore a regime-labeled dictionary, not a universal identification. It is meant for stationary weak and moderate correspondence work, together with strong stationary targets where the stationary pullback is already known. It is not a claim of full nonlinear metric closure in every setting.

## Kerr as the sharpest stationary target

The strongest stationary target currently available is Kerr. Its importance is not merely that it is a famous solution. Its importance is that it makes the scalar and shift split impossible to ignore.

In geometrized units, the exact stationary Kerr pullback gives

$$
G_K = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta\Sigma}{\mathcal A_K}},
\qquad
\beta_K^\phi = -\frac{2mar}{\mathcal A_K},
$$

where $G_K$ is the lapse-like scalar well and $\beta_K^\phi$ is the azimuthal shift.

This is an unusually valuable overlap result. It shows that the M1 gravity grammar does not merely accommodate static spherical wells. It also accommodates stationary rotation in a way that preserves the distinction between scalar clock dressing and directional transport. Kerr is therefore best read, in the present stage of the theory, as an exact stationary target for the scalar-well plus shift grammar.

What is open is not the stationary target itself. What remains open is the full native rotating-source derivation that would produce the Kerr package directly from the M1 field layer without leaning on the stationary GR pullback.

## What this chapter has established, and what it has not

This chapter has established the main achieved field package of Part III.

It has defined the scalar well and shift variables the reader needs. It has stated the minimal stationary equation block. It has shown the Newtonian limit and the sign locks that make the scalar channel readable. It has given the narrow stationary dictionary that connects the M1 field variables to the familiar metric-facing language. And it has shown why Kerr is the strongest current stationary target of the package.

That is a substantial result, but it is not full gravity completion. Detailed coefficient audits, Green-function derivations, stronger gauge-comparison work, and longer stationary solves belong in Appendix III.A and Appendix III.B. Full nonlinear closure, native rotating-source completion, and full tensor and radiative dynamics remain beyond the scope of this chapter.

The next task is therefore not to add more field equations. It is to ask how the stationary field that has now been built is actually read locally by matter and observers.
